The **North Queensland Cowboys** are an Australian professional [rugby league](/wiki/Rugby_league "Rugby league") football club based in [Townsville](/wiki/Townsville "Townsville"), the largest city in [North Queensland](/wiki/North_Queensland "North Queensland"). They compete in Australia's premier rugby league competition, the [National Rugby League](/wiki/National_Rugby_League "National Rugby League") (NRL).

Since their foundation in 1995, the club has appeared in three grand finals ([2005](/wiki/2005_NRL_Grand_Final "2005 NRL Grand Final"), [2015](/wiki/2015_NRL_Grand_Final "2015 NRL Grand Final") and [2017](/wiki/2017_NRL_Grand_Final "2017 NRL Grand Final")) winning in 2015, and has reached the finals twelve times. The team's management headquarters and home ground, [North Queensland Stadium](/wiki/North_Queensland_Stadium "North Queensland Stadium"), currently known as Queensland Country Bank Stadium due to sponsorship rights, are located in the suburb of [South Townsville](/wiki/South_Townsville,_Queensland "South Townsville, Queensland"). Their [women's team](/wiki/North_Queensland_Cowboys_Women "North Queensland Cowboys Women"), established in 2023, currently compete in the [NRL Women's Premiership](/wiki/NRL_Women%27s_Premiership "NRL Women's Premiership").

The Cowboys were admitted to the premiership for the [1995 ARL season](/wiki/1995_ARL_season "1995 ARL season"). They played in the breakaway [Super League](/wiki/Super_League_(Australia) "Super League (Australia)") competition in 1997 before continuing to compete in the re-unified NRL competition the following year. After running into financial trouble in 2001, the club was taken over by [News Limited](/wiki/News_Limited "News Limited"). In 2007, the team was sold by News Limited to the Cowboys Leagues Club.

In 2015, the Cowboys played in the first [all-Queensland grand final](/wiki/2015_NRL_Grand_Final "2015 NRL Grand Final"), defeating the [Brisbane Broncos](/wiki/Brisbane_Broncos "Brisbane Broncos") 17–16 in [golden point](/wiki/Golden_point "Golden point") to win their first, and to date only, premiership.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=North_Queensland_Cowboys&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Performance_Chart_NRL_NQL.svg/250px-Performance_Chart_NRL_NQL.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Performance_Chart_NRL_NQL.svg)

Chart of yearly table positions for North Queensland Cowboys in First Grade Rugby League

Further information: [History of the North Queensland Cowboys](/wiki/History_of_the_North_Queensland_Cowboys "History of the North Queensland Cowboys")

### 1995–1996: ARL

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=North_Queensland_Cowboys&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: 1995–1996: ARL")]

See also: [North Queensland Cowboys season 1995](/wiki/North_Queensland_Cowboys_season_1995 "North Queensland Cowboys season 1995") and [North Queensland Cowboys season 1996](/wiki/North_Queensland_Cowboys_season_1996 "North Queensland Cowboys season 1996")

With the success of the Broncos in 1988, speculation intensified as to if the NSWRL would admit a new team based in North Queensland. In 1993, the NSWRL announced that North Queensland would enter the competition in 1995, along with three other new sides. One of the major difficulties that faced the club in their early years was attracting followers from the more established Queensland-based [Winfield Cup](/wiki/Winfield_Cup "Winfield Cup") side, the [Brisbane Broncos](/wiki/Brisbane_Broncos "Brisbane Broncos"). This was exacerbated by an initial lack of onfield success and stability. In their first two seasons, the Cowboys had eight different captains and finished last in their inaugural season.

### 1997: Super League

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=North_Queensland_Cowboys&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 1997: Super League")]

See also: [North Queensland Cowboys 1997](/wiki/North_Queensland_Cowboys_1997 "North Queensland Cowboys 1997")

After much court action in 1995 and 1996, a ten team Super League competition was held in 1997. The Cowboys competed in this competition, and their squad was bolstered by a number of new signings including [Ian Roberts](/wiki/Ian_Roberts_(rugby_league) "Ian Roberts (rugby league)") and [Steve Walters](/wiki/Steve_Walters "Steve Walters"). However, they were unable to improve on the club's results in previous years, and for the second time in three seasons they were to finish the season in last place.

North Queensland's first game of the Super League season, a 24–16 win over new team the [Adelaide Rams](/wiki/Adelaide_Rams "Adelaide Rams") played on 1 March at Stockland Stadium in front of 17,738 fans was also the first ever game of the Super League's competition.

### 1998–1999: National Rugby League

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=North_Queensland_Cowboys&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 1998–1999: National Rugby League")]

See also: [North Queensland Cowboys season 1998](/wiki/North_Queensland_Cowboys_season_1998 "North Queensland Cowboys season 1998") and [North Queensland Cowboys season 1999](/wiki/North_Queensland_Cowboys_season_1999 "North Queensland Cowboys season 1999")

In 1998 the Super League and Australian Rugby League competitions merged to form the National Rugby League (NRL). The Cowboys began their first season in this competition strongly, and after six rounds they were in equal first place. Although they fell away later in the season, they were to record the largest come-back to date in an Australian first grade rugby league match, defeating the [Penrith Panthers](/wiki/Penrith_Panthers "Penrith Panthers") 36–28 after trailing 26–0 at half-time. 1998 also saw the Cowboys record their largest loss to date, being defeated 62–0 by the [North Sydney Bears](/wiki/North_Sydney_Bears "North Sydney Bears") in the last round of the home and away season.

The Cowboys signed their eleventh captain in 1999, [Noel Goldthorpe](/wiki/Noel_Goldthorpe "Noel Goldthorpe"). [Paul Bowman](/wiki/Paul_Bowman_(rugby_league) "Paul Bowman (rugby league)") was also to serve in that role during the season. Although their on-field performances were not spectacular, continuing high attendance figures saw aggregate attendances exceed one million spectators. This season the Cowboys also provided their first State of Origin representative when [Paul Green](/wiki/Paul_Green_(rugby_league) "Paul Green (rugby league)") was selected as Queensland's halfback for game 2 of the [1999 State of Origin series](/wiki/1999_State_of_Origin_series "1999 State of Origin series").

### 2000–2003: Improved results

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=North_Queensland_Cowboys&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2000–2003: Improved results")]

See also: [North Queensland Cowboys season 2000](/wiki/North_Queensland_Cowboys_season_2000 "North Queensland Cowboys season 2000"), [North Queensland Cowboys season 2001](/wiki/North_Queensland_Cowboys_season_2001 "North Queensland Cowboys season 2001"), [North Queensland Cowboys season 2002](/wiki/North_Queensland_Cowboys_season_2002 "North Queensland Cowboys season 2002"), and [North Queensland Cowboys season 2003](/wiki/North_Queensland_Cowboys_season_2003 "North Queensland Cowboys season 2003")

In the years 2000 through to 2002 the Cowboys continued to struggle with off-field dramas and the struggles of coaches
and poor on-field performances. After finishing last in 2000, season 2001 began slowly. [Tim Sheens](/wiki/Tim_Sheens "Tim Sheens") resigned on 25 May and was replaced by his then assistant [Murray Hurst](/wiki/Murray_Hurst "Murray Hurst"). 4 straight losses in the opening rounds of 2002 led to Hurst being replaced early in the 2002 season, by former Illawarra Steelers and Leeds Rhinos coach [Graham Murray](/wiki/Graham_Murray "Graham Murray"). Murray stamped his authority and coaching prowess on the club and the NQ Cowboys looked far more competitive towards the end of the 2002 season. The Cowboys spent much of the 2003 season in the top eight with much improved performances from a host of players, including local talents Matt Bowen and Josh Hannay. The 2003 season ended with the Cowboys four points adrift of a top eight play-off position.

### 2004–2005: First time finalists and Grand Finalists

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=North_Queensland_Cowboys&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: 2004–2005: First time finalists and Grand Finalists")]

See also: [North Queensland Cowboys season 2004](/wiki/North_Queensland_Cowboys_season_2004 "North Queensland Cowboys season 2004") and [North Queensland Cowboys season 2005](/wiki/North_Queensland_Cowboys_season_2005 "North Queensland Cowboys season 2005")

After a slow start to the season that saw them at 13th on the ladder with just one win and five losses, the Cowboys turned it around in the second half of the season to finish with 12 wins and 11 losses and 7th spot, giving the club their first ever top eight appearance.

They upset the 2nd place [Canterbury](/wiki/Canterbury_Bulldogs "Canterbury Bulldogs") away from home in the first week of the finals, 30–22, thanks largely to hat-trick hero [Matt Sing](/wiki/Matt_Sing "Matt Sing"). The following week the Cowboys defeated their state rivals the [Brisbane Broncos](/wiki/Brisbane_Broncos "Brisbane Broncos") at home, 10–0, in perhaps the club's most famous victory.

They ended up falling one game short of the grand final, losing to the [Sydney Roosters](/wiki/Sydney_Roosters "Sydney Roosters"), 19–16.

North Queensland would go one better in 2005, when they reached their first grand final in club history. With the help of new recruits [Carl Webb](/wiki/Carl_Webb "Carl Webb") and [Johnathan Thurston](/wiki/Johnathan_Thurston "Johnathan Thurston"), the side finished in 5th spot and with back-to-back finals appearances. They would ultimately lose the grand final to the [Wests Tigers](/wiki/Wests_Tigers "Wests Tigers").

In his first year with the club, [Johnathan Thurston](/wiki/Johnathan_Thurston "Johnathan Thurston") won the [Dally M Medal](/wiki/Dally_M_Medal "Dally M Medal") and made his State of Origin debut for [Queensland](/wiki/Queensland_Maroons "Queensland Maroons").

### 2006–2007: Further improvement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=North_Queensland_Cowboys&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: 2006–2007: Further improvement")]

See also: [North Queensland Cowboys season 2006](/wiki/North_Queensland_Cowboys_season_2006 "North Queensland Cowboys season 2006") and [North Queensland Cowboys season 2007](/wiki/North_Queensland_Cowboys_season_2007 "North Queensland Cowboys season 2007")

In 2006, the Cowboys started the year with a 6-game winning streak and looked destined for another finals appearance, before ending the season with just 5 wins from 19 games and finishing in 9th position.

The 2007 season saw the Cowboys 3rd finals appearance and their first top 4 finish. They faced the [Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs](/wiki/Canterbury-Bankstown_Bulldogs "Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs") in Townsville in week 1 of the finals, scraping home by 2 points. The following week, they defeated the [New Zealand Warriors](/wiki/New_Zealand_Warriors "New Zealand Warriors") at home by 37 points. They fell one game short of the grand final again, this time losing to [Manly](/wiki/Manly_Sea_Eagles "Manly Sea Eagles"), 28–6. 2007 saw retirement of club legend [Paul Bowman](/wiki/Paul_Bowman_(rugby_league) "Paul Bowman (rugby league)"), who took up a coaching role with the team in 2008.

Johnathan Thurston finished the year with his second Dally M Medal and Matt Bowen was named Dally M Fullback of the Year.

### 2008–2010: Struggling seasons

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=North_Queensland_Cowboys&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: 2008–2010: Struggling seasons")]

See also: [North Queensland Cowboys season 2008](/wiki/North_Queensland_Cowboys_season_2008 "North Queensland Cowboys season 2008"), [North Queensland Cowboys season 2009](/wiki/North_Queensland_Cowboys_season_2009 "North Queensland Cowboys season 2009"), and [2010 North Queensland Cowboys season](/wiki/2010_North_Queensland_Cowboys_season "2010 North Queensland Cowboys season")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/b/b8/Lukeodonnellfanday2008.jpg/250px-Lukeodonnellfanday2008.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lukeodonnellfanday2008.jpg)

Former Cowboys' lock Luke O'Donnell

At the start of the 2008 season, the club announced head coach [Graham Murray](/wiki/Graham_Murray "Graham Murray")'s contract would not be renewed. After only winning three of the first ten games, Murray resigned from his job and the club promoted assistant coach [Ian Millward](/wiki/Ian_Millward "Ian Millward") to the head coaching role for the rest of the year. During his stint as head coach, the club could only win two more games. Throughout the season the team were decimated by injuries and suspensions as well as off-field issues. In the end, the Cowboys finished 15th on the ladder avoiding the wooden spoon by for and against.

2009 saw the appointment of [Neil Henry](/wiki/Neil_Henry "Neil Henry") as head coach, who had won the Dally M Coach of the Year with the [Canberra Raiders](/wiki/Canberra_Raiders "Canberra Raiders") the previous year. The club however experienced another disappointing season, finishing 12th with 11 wins and 13 losses. 2009 also saw [Matthew Bowen](/wiki/Matthew_Bowen "Matthew Bowen") score his 100th try, becoming the first North Queensland player to score 100 tries for the club and the debut of future Australian and New South Wales representative forward [James Tamou](/wiki/James_Tamou "James Tamou").

The beginning out the 2010 season was marred with the speculation of whether [Johnathan Thurston](/wiki/Johnathan_Thurston "Johnathan Thurston") would renew his contract with the club. Thurston ended up re-signing for another three seasons, however it brought very little success. The club finished 15th and would have received the wooden spoon if not for the [Melbourne Storm](/wiki/Melbourne_Storm "Melbourne Storm") salary cap scandal. 2010 saw the emergence of [Scott Bolton](/wiki/Scott_Bolton_(rugby_league) "Scott Bolton (rugby league)") and seeing the test debut of [Matthew Scott](/wiki/Matthew_Scott_(rugby_league) "Matthew Scott (rugby league)"). [Jason Taumalolo](/wiki/Jason_Taumalolo "Jason Taumalolo") became the Cowboys youngest ever debutant, making his debut against the [Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs](/wiki/Canterbury-Bankstown_Bulldogs "Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs") in round 24 aged 17 years and 82 days.

### 2011–2013: Return to the Finals

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=North_Queensland_Cowboys&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: 2011–2013: Return to the Finals")]

See also: [2011 North Queensland Cowboys season](/wiki/2011_North_Queensland_Cowboys_season "2011 North Queensland Cowboys season"), [2012 North Queensland Cowboys season](/wiki/2012_North_Queensland_Cowboys_season "2012 North Queensland Cowboys season"), and [2013 North Queensland Cowboys season](/wiki/2013_North_Queensland_Cowboys_season "2013 North Queensland Cowboys season")

After missing the finals for the last 3 seasons the Cowboys underwent a major overhaul of personnel for the 2011 season. Club legend [Ty Williams](/wiki/Ty_Williams "Ty Williams") retired and they parted ways with club favourites [Luke O'Donnell](/wiki/Luke_O%27Donnell "Luke O'Donnell"), [Carl Webb](/wiki/Carl_Webb "Carl Webb") and [Steve Southern](/wiki/Steve_Southern "Steve Southern"). Their biggest signing for the 2011 season was [Queensland](/wiki/Queensland_rugby_league_team "Queensland rugby league team") and [Australian](/wiki/Australian_national_rugby_league_team "Australian national rugby league team") representative centre [Brent Tate](/wiki/Brent_Tate "Brent Tate"). They also made key recruits in the signings of former [Melbourne Storm](/wiki/Melbourne_Storm "Melbourne Storm") and [Queensland State of Origin](/wiki/Queensland_rugby_league_team "Queensland rugby league team") representative [Dallas Johnson](/wiki/Dallas_Johnson "Dallas Johnson") and premiership winner [Glenn Hall](/wiki/Glenn_Hall_(rugby_league) "Glenn Hall (rugby league)") from the English Super League, re-signing a former Cowboy in [Gavin Cooper](/wiki/Gavin_Cooper "Gavin Cooper") and picking up younger, experienced first graders such as [Antonio Winterstein](/wiki/Antonio_Winterstein "Antonio Winterstein").

The Cowboys' new signings had a positive impact on the NRL team, with the Cowboys spending much of the year in the top four before a late slide dropped them to seventh position and giving them their first finals appearance since 2007. That year, they were the most watched NRL club on pay television, and their round four clash with the [Parramatta Eels](/wiki/Parramatta_Eels "Parramatta Eels") was the fourth most-watched sports event in Fox Sport's history.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

2012 saw North Queensland return to the finals after finishing in 5th spot. They faced the [Brisbane Broncos](/wiki/Brisbane_Broncos "Brisbane Broncos") in the first week of the finals, winning 33–16, with halfback [Michael Morgan](/wiki/Michael_Morgan_(rugby_league_born_1991) "Michael Morgan (rugby league born 1991)") scoring a hat trick. They lost the following week to Manly, in controversial circumstances. Club legend [Aaron Payne](/wiki/Aaron_Payne "Aaron Payne") retired at the end of the season, after ten years with the team.

North Queensland again made the finals in 2013, the first time the club had made the finals series three straight years, when they finished in 8th place. After a poor start to the season, the side went on a 6-game winning streak following the sacking of coach [Neil Henry](/wiki/Neil_Henry "Neil Henry"). They were eliminated in Week 1 of the finals, after a controversial loss to the [Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks](/wiki/Cronulla-Sutherland_Sharks "Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks"). Club legend [Matthew Bowen](/wiki/Matthew_Bowen "Matthew Bowen") played his last season in the NRL, announcing he would be taking up an offer with Super League side, the [Wigan Warriors](/wiki/Wigan_Warriors "Wigan Warriors"). Club stalwarts [Ashley Graham](/wiki/Ashley_Graham_(rugby_league) "Ashley Graham (rugby league)") and [Dallas Johnson](/wiki/Dallas_Johnson "Dallas Johnson") also announced their retirements.

On 1 October 2013, former North Queensland halfback [Paul Green](/wiki/Paul_Green_(rugby_league) "Paul Green (rugby league)") was announced as head coach for the 2014 and 2015 seasons, becoming the first former Cowboys player to coach the club.

### 2014–2017: A new era and the club's first premiership

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=North_Queensland_Cowboys&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: 2014–2017: A new era and the club's first premiership")]

See also: [2014 North Queensland Cowboys season](/wiki/2014_North_Queensland_Cowboys_season "2014 North Queensland Cowboys season"), [2015 North Queensland Cowboys season](/wiki/2015_North_Queensland_Cowboys_season "2015 North Queensland Cowboys season"), [2016 North Queensland Cowboys season](/wiki/2016_North_Queensland_Cowboys_season "2016 North Queensland Cowboys season"), and [2017 North Queensland Cowboys season](/wiki/2017_North_Queensland_Cowboys_season "2017 North Queensland Cowboys season")

The Cowboys started their 2014 season by winning the inaugural [NRL Auckland Nines](/wiki/NRL_Auckland_Nines "NRL Auckland Nines") tournament which was held at [Eden Park](/wiki/Eden_Park "Eden Park") in [Auckland](/wiki/Auckland "Auckland"). After losing key signing [Lachlan Coote](/wiki/Lachlan_Coote "Lachlan Coote") early in the tournament to injury, the Cowboys went on to face the [Brisbane Broncos](/wiki/Brisbane_Broncos "Brisbane Broncos") in the final, defeating them 16–7 to claim the trophy and a prize money cheque of $370,000.

After a difficult start to the season and a string of injuries to key players including internationals, the North Queensland club made a late charge to the finals finishing in fifth place and cementing a spot in the finals where they defeated the [Brisbane Broncos](/wiki/Brisbane_Broncos "Brisbane Broncos"), 32–20. They then travelled to Sydney to face the 2013 premiers the [Sydney Roosters](/wiki/Sydney_Roosters "Sydney Roosters") in the second week of the finals where they were defeated 31–30.

In 2015, North Queensland experienced their best ever regular season. They had a club record 11 game winning streak and finished with a club record 17 wins. In September, [Johnathan Thurston](/wiki/Johnathan_Thurston "Johnathan Thurston") won a record fourth [Dally M Medal](/wiki/Dally_M_Medal "Dally M Medal") at the Dally M Awards.

On 26 September 2015, North Queensland qualified for their second grand final defeating the [Melbourne Storm](/wiki/Melbourne_Storm "Melbourne Storm"), 32–12. In the [Grand Final](/wiki/2015_NRL_Grand_Final "2015 NRL Grand Final"), they defeated the [Brisbane Broncos](/wiki/Brisbane_Broncos "Brisbane Broncos"), 17–16. Thurston, who won the [Clive Churchill Medal](/wiki/Clive_Churchill_Medal "Clive Churchill Medal"), kicked the winning field goal in [golden point](/wiki/Golden_point "Golden point") to win North Queensland's their first ever premiership.

On 21 February 2016, the club won their first [World Club Challenge](/wiki/2016_World_Club_Series#World_Club_Challenge "2016 World Club Series") and were crowned the world's best club, defeating the [Leeds Rhinos](/wiki/Leeds_Rhinos "Leeds Rhinos") 38–4 at [Headingley Rugby Stadium](/wiki/Headingley_Rugby_Stadium "Headingley Rugby Stadium") in Leeds, England. The club could not defend its premiership, finishing the [2016 NRL season](/wiki/2016_NRL_season "2016 NRL season") in 4th place and falling one game short of the Grand Final, being eliminated by the [Cronulla Sharks](/wiki/Cronulla_Sharks "Cronulla Sharks") 32–20 at [Allianz Stadium](/wiki/Sydney_Football_Stadium_(1988) "Sydney Football Stadium (1988)").

In 2017, despite losing co-captains [Johnathan Thurston](/wiki/Johnathan_Thurston "Johnathan Thurston") and [Matthew Scott](/wiki/Matthew_Scott_(rugby_league) "Matthew Scott (rugby league)") to season-ending injuries, the club qualified for their third [Grand Final](/wiki/2017_NRL_Grand_Final "2017 NRL Grand Final"). Although they were just the second side to reach the Grand Final from 8th place, they would lose to the [Melbourne Storm](/wiki/Melbourne_Storm "Melbourne Storm"), who finished the regular season as minor premiers.

### 2018–2019: Departure of club legends

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=North_Queensland_Cowboys&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: 2018–2019: Departure of club legends")]

See also: [2018 North Queensland Cowboys season](/wiki/2018_North_Queensland_Cowboys_season "2018 North Queensland Cowboys season") and [2019 North Queensland Cowboys season](/wiki/2019_North_Queensland_Cowboys_season "2019 North Queensland Cowboys season")

Before the 2018 season began, North Queensland were predicted by many to challenge for the premiership with the return of [Matthew Scott](/wiki/Matthew_Scott_(rugby_league) "Matthew Scott (rugby league)") and [Johnathan Thurston](/wiki/Johnathan_Thurston "Johnathan Thurston") from injury.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Instead, they struggled to move away from the bottom of the ladder, going on two separate five-game losing streaks throughout the season. With two rounds to play, the club sat in last place. Two wins from their final two games saw them finish in 13th. The 2018 season would be the last for Thurston, who retired after 14 seasons, 294 games and one premiership for the club. In his final match, the Cowboys defeated the Titans 30–26.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

Despite a poor 2018 and the loss of Thurston, many expected the Cowboys to return to the finals in 2019.[[4]](#cite_note-4) What followed was another disappointing year in which the club finished 14th. Club legends [Matthew Scott](/wiki/Matthew_Scott_(rugby_league) "Matthew Scott (rugby league)") and [Scott Bolton](/wiki/Scott_Bolton_(rugby_league) "Scott Bolton (rugby league)") were farewelled at the end of the season. Both were members of the club's premiership winning side in 2015 and spent their entire NRL careers at the club.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6) The club also played its final game at [Willows Sports Complex](/wiki/Willows_Sports_Complex "Willows Sports Complex"), defeating the [Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs](/wiki/Canterbury-Bankstown_Bulldogs "Canterbury-Bankstown Bulldogs") 15–8.[[7]](#cite_note-7)

### 2020–2022: Rebuilding seasons and finals return

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=North_Queensland_Cowboys&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: 2020–2022: Rebuilding seasons and finals return")]

See also: [2020 North Queensland Cowboys season](/wiki/2020_North_Queensland_Cowboys_season "2020 North Queensland Cowboys season"), [2021 North Queensland Cowboys season](/wiki/2021_North_Queensland_Cowboys_season "2021 North Queensland Cowboys season"), and [2022 North Queensland Cowboys season](/wiki/2022_North_Queensland_Cowboys_season "2022 North Queensland Cowboys season")

Following two poor seasons in which they missed the finals, North Queensland looked to rebuild ahead of the [2020 NRL season](/wiki/2020_NRL_season "2020 NRL season"). Their marquee signing was [Australian](/wiki/Australian_Kangaroos "Australian Kangaroos") and [Queensland](/wiki/Queensland_Maroons "Queensland Maroons") representative [Valentine Holmes](/wiki/Valentine_Holmes "Valentine Holmes"), who had spent 2019 playing for the [New York Jets](/wiki/New_York_Jets "New York Jets") in the [NFL](/wiki/NFL "NFL"). Holmes, a [Townsville](/wiki/Townsville,_Queensland "Townsville, Queensland") junior, signed a six-year deal with the club.[[8]](#cite_note-8)

On 20 July 2020, after just three wins from their first 10 games, Paul Green announced his resignation from the club, with assistant coach [Josh Hannay](/wiki/Josh_Hannay "Josh Hannay") named as the interim head coach.[[9]](#cite_note-9) On 4 September 2020, [Todd Payten](/wiki/Todd_Payten "Todd Payten"), a former assistant coach at the club, was announced as North Queensland's head coach on a three-year deal, starting in 2021.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Under Payten, the Cowboys endured another disappointing season, finishing 15th with seven wins.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

In 2022, the club returned to the finals series after four seasons, finishing the regular season in third and equalling a club record of 17 regular season wins.[[12]](#cite_note-12) They ultimately fell just one game short of the [Grand Final](/wiki/2022_NRL_Grand_Final "2022 NRL Grand Final"), with Todd Payten later named the [Dally M](/wiki/Dally_M_Medal "Dally M Medal") Coach of the Year.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

North Queensland started the 2023 NRL season poorly which saw the club only winning three of their opening ten games. In round 12, the club suffered one of their worst defeats losing 66–18 against the Wests Tigers. In round 18, North Queensland recorded the third biggest victory in Australian rugby league history defeating the Wests Tigers 74–0. It was also the largest victory of the NRL era.[[14]](#cite_note-14)
North Queensland would finish the 2023 NRL season in 11th place and miss the finals. In the final round of the season, the club had the chance to make the finals if they were able to beat Penrith but the North Queensland side came up short losing 44–12.[[15]](#cite_note-15)
In the [2024 NRL season](/wiki/2024_NRL_season "2024 NRL season"), North Queensland would finish 5th on the table and qualify for the finals. After defeating Newcastle in the elimination final, the clubs season would be ended the following week by Cronulla in the semi-final.[[16]](#cite_note-16)
In the [2025 NRL season](/wiki/2025_NRL_season "2025 NRL season"), North Queensland endured a difficult campaign finishing 12th on the table where they managed to record only nine wins all year.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

#### Cowboys Hall of Fame

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=North_Queensland_Cowboys&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Cowboys Hall of Fame")]

In 2015, the Cowboys launched their official Hall of Fame during their 20th anniversary celebrations. The foundation criteria were that any players considered had to have played at least 100 NRL games for the club and that they had to have been retired for at least two years. The inductees were decided by a specially selected panel, including members of the Cowboys' organisation and the wider rugby league community, and are assessed on their skill and ability, leadership, integrity and character, their state and international honours, and awards won at the club.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

In 2019, [Matthew Bowen](/wiki/Matthew_Bowen "Matthew Bowen") was inducted into the Hall of Fame at the club's 2019 season launch event.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

## Honours

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=North_Queensland_Cowboys&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Honours")]

See also: [List of North Queensland Cowboys honours](/wiki/List_of_North_Queensland_Cowboys_honours "List of North Queensland Cowboys honours")

At the end of each season the North Queensland Cowboys hold their Presentation Ball, in which they present (among other awards) the [Paul Bowman](/wiki/Paul_Bowman_(rugby_league) "Paul Bowman (rugby league)") Medal for Cowboy of the Year. The player of the year award, which was named after Bowman in 2007, is awarded to Cowboys player voted as the best and fairest over the premiership season. After each match, the coaching staff award three votes to the best Cowboys player on ground, two votes to the second best, and one vote to the third best, similar to the [Dally M Medal](/wiki/Dally_M_Medal "Dally M Medal") voting system.

The most recent Paul Bowman Medal winner is [Jason Taumalolo](/wiki/Jason_Taumalolo "Jason Taumalolo"), who won the award for the first time in 2016 and is the first player to win the award four consecutive times.[[25]](#cite_note-25) Bowman himself is a two-time winner of the award (1999, 2000), as is [Luke O'Donnell](/wiki/Luke_O%27Donnell "Luke O'Donnell") (2004, 2009), [Aaron Payne](/wiki/Aaron_Payne "Aaron Payne") (2006, 2008) and [Matt Scott](/wiki/Matthew_Scott_(rugby_league) "Matthew Scott (rugby league)") (2010, 2013), while [Johnathan Thurston](/wiki/Johnathan_Thurston "Johnathan Thurston"), was the first player to win the award four times (2005, 2012, 2014, 2015). The first recipient of the medal was [Wayne Sing](/wiki/Wayne_Sing "Wayne Sing") in 1995.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

In 2014, the Cowboys won their first major trophy, the inaugural [NRL Auckland Nines](/wiki/NRL_Auckland_Nines "NRL Auckland Nines") tournament which was held at [Eden Park](/wiki/Eden_Park "Eden Park") in [Auckland](/wiki/Auckland "Auckland"), defeating the [Brisbane Broncos](/wiki/Brisbane_Broncos "Brisbane Broncos") 16–7 in the final. A year later the Cowboys won their [first NRL premiership](/wiki/2015_NRL_Grand_Final "2015 NRL Grand Final"), defeating the Broncos 17–16 in [golden point](/wiki/Golden_point "Golden point") extra time. [Johnathan Thurston](/wiki/Johnathan_Thurston "Johnathan Thurston") was awarded the [Clive Churchill Medal](/wiki/Clive_Churchill_Medal "Clive Churchill Medal").

In 2020, the Cowboys won the [NRL Nines](/wiki/2020_NRL_Nines "2020 NRL Nines") for the second time, becoming the first club to do so. They defeated the [St George Illawarra Dragons](/wiki/St_George_Illawarra_Dragons "St George Illawarra Dragons") 23–14 in the final with [Scott Drinkwater](/wiki/Scott_Drinkwater "Scott Drinkwater") being named the Player of the Tournament.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

## Statistics and records

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=North_Queensland_Cowboys&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Statistics and records")]

Further information: [List of North Queensland Cowboys records](/wiki/List_of_North_Queensland_Cowboys_records "List of North Queensland Cowboys records")

After a 10-year finals drought, the Cowboys made their first finals appearance in 2004, finishing one game away from the grand final. The following year the team played in their first grand final, losing to the [Wests Tigers](/wiki/Wests_Tigers "Wests Tigers"). The North Queensland club's second appearance in the grand final would not come for another 10 years, when they met Queensland rivals, [Brisbane Broncos](/wiki/Brisbane_Broncos "Brisbane Broncos"), in the 2015 decider. In one of the most entertaining grand finals in living memory, [Johnathan Thurston](/wiki/Johnathan_Thurston "Johnathan Thurston") kicked a golden-point field goal to win the Cowboys their maiden premiership.

The club's [Under 20s](/wiki/National_Youth_Competition_(rugby_league) "National Youth Competition (rugby league)") side finished runners-up in the [2011 NYC grand final](/wiki/2011_NRL_Under-20s_season "2011 NRL Under-20s season") to the [New Zealand Warriors](/wiki/New_Zealand_Warriors "New Zealand Warriors") while their feeder teams have won the [Queensland Cup](/wiki/Queensland_Cup "Queensland Cup") four times. In 2005 with the [North Queensland Young Guns](/wiki/North_Queensland_Young_Guns "North Queensland Young Guns"), 2010 and 2014 with the [Northern Pride](/wiki/Northern_Pride_RLFC "Northern Pride RLFC") and 2013 with the [Mackay Cutters](/wiki/Mackay_Cutters "Mackay Cutters").

Fullback [Matthew Bowen](/wiki/Matthew_Bowen "Matthew Bowen") currently holds the record for most first grade games and most tries for club. Bowen also holds the club record for most tries in a season, with 22 in 2007. [Paul Bowman](/wiki/Paul_Bowman_(rugby_league) "Paul Bowman (rugby league)") became the first Cowboy to play 200 first grade games in 2007. [Johnathan Thurston](/wiki/Johnathan_Thurston "Johnathan Thurston") jointly holds the record for most points in a match with [Josh Hannay](/wiki/Josh_Hannay "Josh Hannay") at 24. Hannay's 10 goals against [South Sydney](/wiki/South_Sydney_Rabbitohs "South Sydney Rabbitohs") in 2003. [Johnathan Thurston](/wiki/Johnathan_Thurston "Johnathan Thurston") currently holds the most points in a season in 2014 with 234 points. Winger Ray Mercy was the first, and so far only, Cowboy to score four tries in a match, which he did against [Balmain](/wiki/Balmain_Tigers "Balmain Tigers") in 1998.

The club's biggest winning margin is 74 points, achieved in 2023 in a 74 - 0 victory over the [Wests Tigers](/wiki/Wests_Tigers "Wests Tigers"). Their heaviest defeat is a 62–0 loss by the [Parramatta Eels](/wiki/Parramatta_Eels "Parramatta Eels") in 2001.

## Stadium

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=North_Queensland_Cowboys&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Stadium")]

Main article: [North Queensland Stadium](/wiki/North_Queensland_Stadium "North Queensland Stadium")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/14-05-2005-dairy_farmers_at_dusk.JPG/250px-14-05-2005-dairy_farmers_at_dusk.JPG)](/wiki/File:14-05-2005-dairy_farmers_at_dusk.JPG)

View of the Cowboys former home ground, Willows Sports Complex

From 1995 to 2019, the Cowboys played their home games at [Willows Sports Complex](/wiki/Willows_Sports_Complex "Willows Sports Complex"), located in the [Townsville](/wiki/Townsville "Townsville") suburb of [Kirwan](/wiki/Kirwan,_Queensland "Kirwan, Queensland"). Prior to 1995, the venue hosted trotting paceway nights, and was the main pacing venue for the Townsville District, but due to the admittance of the Cowboys, the venue was turned into a rugby league stadium.

The venue was renamed Stockland Stadium in 1995, due to sponsorship rights, and attracted a crowd of 23,156 to the Cowboys first ever premiership game. In 1998, the name was changed to Malanda Stadium by new sponsors Dairy Farmers, before being renamed again in 1999 to Dairy Farmers Stadium. The stadium underwent major renovations in 2005 and 2006, with the eastern, northern and southern grandstands receiving upgrades, the addition of more corporate boxes and a new replay screen and public address system installed. Dairy Farmers 15-year partnership with the Cowboys ended at the end of 2012 season, as the club looked for a new naming rights sponsor for the stadium.[[28]](#cite_note-28)

In 2013, the stadium was renamed 1300SMILES Stadium, after the Townsville-based dental practice entered into a five-year sponsorship deal with Stadiums Queensland.[[29]](#cite_note-29) On 29 August 2019, the club played their final game at the stadium, defeating the [Canterbury Bulldogs](/wiki/Canterbury_Bulldogs "Canterbury Bulldogs") 15–8.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

The club record attendance for a regular season match is 30,302, set against the Brisbane Broncos in the round 8 of the 1999 season. The record attendance for a finals match is 24,989, set against the Brisbane Broncos in week 2 of the 2004 finals series.

In 2020, the club moved to the new 25,000-seat **[Queensland Country Bank Stadium](/wiki/Queensland_Country_Bank_Stadium "Queensland Country Bank Stadium")** in [South Townsville](/wiki/South_Townsville,_Queensland "South Townsville, Queensland").[[31]](#cite_note-31)

## Emblems and colours

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=North_Queensland_Cowboys&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Emblems and colours")]

Before becoming the Cowboys, the team names Marlins, Stingrays and the very popular Crocodiles were tossed up as potential team logos for the side, before public consultation resulted in the name 'Cowboys' (the Crocodiles name would eventually go to Townsville's [National Basketball League](/wiki/National_Basketball_League_(Australasia) "National Basketball League (Australasia)") team, who changed their name to the [Townsville Crocodiles](/wiki/Townsville_Crocodiles "Townsville Crocodiles") in 1998 after a dispute with the [NBA](/wiki/NBA "NBA")'s [Phoenix Suns](/wiki/Phoenix_Suns "Phoenix Suns") over their original name, the Townsville Suns).[[33]](#cite_note-33) The colours navy blue and yellow were chosen to reflect the colours of the Townsville representative sides. Grey was also added as primary colour with white introduced as a secondary colour.

## Rivalries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=North_Queensland_Cowboys&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: Rivalries")]

### Brisbane Broncos

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=North_Queensland_Cowboys&action=edit&section=37 "Edit section: Brisbane Broncos")]

Main article: [Queensland derby](/wiki/Queensland_derby "Queensland derby")

North Queensland's main rivals are the [Brisbane Broncos](/wiki/Brisbane_Broncos "Brisbane Broncos"). From 1999 to 2006, the two clubs were the only Queensland-based sides in the [NRL](/wiki/NRL "NRL"). A one-side rivalry for nearly 10 years, the Brisbane side had not lost to North Queensland in 16 encounters. In 2004, the teams met in the finals for the first time, with North Queensland defeating Brisbane 10–0, to record their first win in the derby.[[46]](#cite_note-46)

In the final round of the [2020 NRL season](/wiki/2020_NRL_season "2020 NRL season"), North Queensland condemned Brisbane to their first ever [Wooden Spoon](/wiki/Wooden_Spoon_(award) "Wooden Spoon (award)"). Brisbane needed to win the match to overtake [Canterbury-Bankstown](/wiki/Canterbury_Bulldogs "Canterbury Bulldogs") but lost the match. As of 2020, the sides have met five more times in the finals, including the [2015 NRL Grand Final](/wiki/2015_NRL_Grand_Final "2015 NRL Grand Final"), the first all-Queensland Grand Final, which North Queensland won 17–16 in extra time.[[47]](#cite_note-47) The rivalry between the two clubs is considered one of the best in modern rugby league.[[48]](#cite_note-48)[[49]](#cite_note-49)[[50]](#cite_note-50)[[51]](#cite_note-51)[[52]](#cite_note-52)

### Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=North_Queensland_Cowboys&action=edit&section=38 "Edit section: Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks")]

The club had a minor competitive rivalry with the [Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks](/wiki/Cronulla-Sutherland_Sharks "Cronulla-Sutherland Sharks")[[53]](#cite_note-53) The games with Cronulla grew from finals football, with the two clubs meeting five times in the finals since 2013.[[54]](#cite_note-54) Since 2023, the player of the match in games between the two clubs has been awarded the Paul Green Medal, in honour of [Paul Green](/wiki/Paul_Green_(rugby_league) "Paul Green (rugby league)"), a former player for both clubs and coach for the Cowboys who died in 2022.[[55]](#cite_note-55)

## Women's team

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=North_Queensland_Cowboys&action=edit&section=39 "Edit section: Women's team")]

Main article: [North Queensland Cowboys Women](/wiki/North_Queensland_Cowboys_Women "North Queensland Cowboys Women")

On 15 June 2022, the Cowboys were admitted to the [NRL Women's Premiership](/wiki/NRL_Women%27s_Premiership "NRL Women's Premiership") for the 2023 season.[[56]](#cite_note-56) On 24 November 2022, [Ben Jeffries](/wiki/Ben_Jeffries "Ben Jeffries") was announced as the club's inaugural NRLW head coach, with Anita Creenaune appointed as Head of Women's Elite Programs.[[57]](#cite_note-57) On 4 April 2023, Mackay's [Emma Manzelmann](/wiki/Emma_Manzelmann "Emma Manzelmann") became the club's inaugural signing.[[58]](#cite_note-58)
